coredata.dc:title: Quick synthesis of 2-propanol derived fluorescent carbon dots for bioimaging applications
coredata.dc:creator: Angamuthu, Raja
Palanisamy, Priya
Vasudevan, Vasanthakumar
Nagarajan, Sedhu
Rajendran, Ramesh
Vairamuthu, Raj
coredata.dc:description: Herein, for the first time, we present a one-pot ingenious preparative method for fluorescent carbon dots from 2-propanol (2P-CDs) without external treatments. Structure, morphology, chemical composition and fluorescence properties of the 2P-CDs were examined. These results confirm that the as-synthesized 2P-CDs are amorphous, monodispersed, spherical and the average particle size is 2.5 ± 0.7 nm. Most importantly, excitation-dependent emission properties were observed, which suggest that these 2P-CDs may be used in multicolor bioimaging applications. When incubated with HeLa cells, the 2P-CDs exhibit low cytotoxicity, and positive biocompatibility. Confocal microscopy image shows the uptake of 2P-CDs by HeLa cells and the application of probable biomarker is demonstrated.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Facile synthesis
Surface morphology
Cytotoxicity
Cell imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Calculation of PL quantum yield of 2P-CDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of 2P-CDs: (a) TEM (inset: SAED pattern) and (b) Particle size distribution histogram.
Characterization of 2P-CDs: (a) FT-IR spectra and (b) XRD Pattern.
Characterization of 2P-CDs: (a) XPS survey spectrum (b) high-resolution XPS spectrum of C 1s peak (c) high-resolution XPS spectrum of O 1s peak and (d) microRaman spectrum.
Characterization of 2P-CDs: (a) EDS spectra and (b) Zeta Potential histogram.
Characterization of 2P-CDs: (a) UV–Vis absorption and emission spectra (inset 2P-CDs images under day light and UV light exposure) and (b) PL spectra.

Confocal microscopy images of HeLa cells treated with 20 μg mL−1 of the 2P-CDs. ((a) blue (465–486), (b) green (508–540), (c) yellow (561–604), (d) red (636–700) excited at wavelength 488 nm; (e) bright field and (f) merged confocal images). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article).

head.ce:title: Quick synthesis of 2-propanol derived fluorescent carbon dots for bioimaging applications

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Advanced Materials Research Laboratory, Department of Chemistry, Periyar University, Salem, 636 011, Tamil Nadu, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Energy and Functional Materials Laboratory, Department of Physics, Periyar University, Salem, 636 011, Tamil Nadu, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Centre for Nanoscience and Nanotechnology, Periyar University, Salem, 636 011, Tamil Nadu, India

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Advanced Materials Research Laboratory, Department of Chemistry, Periyar University, Salem, 636 011, Tamil Nadu, India.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, for the first time, we present a one-pot ingenious preparative method for fluorescent carbon dots from 2-propanol (2P-CDs) without external treatments. Structure, morphology, chemical composition and fluorescence properties of the 2P-CDs were examined. These results confirm that the as-synthesized 2P-CDs are amorphous, monodispersed, spherical and the average particle size is 2.5 ± 0.7 nm. Most importantly, excitation-dependent emission properties were observed, which suggest that these 2P-CDs may be used in multicolor bioimaging applications. When incubated with HeLa cells, the 2P-CDs exhibit low cytotoxicity, and positive biocompatibility. Confocal microscopy image shows the uptake of 2P-CDs by HeLa cells and the application of probable biomarker is demonstrated.

ce:section.ce:para: In this decade, luminous nanomaterials have many interested applications in biological screening, chemical sensing, and other related fields. Fluorescent carbon nanomaterials, mainly carbon dots, carbon nanoparticles and graphene quantum dots generate extensive attention for a large array of promising applications. Based on the carbon skeleton structure, typically exist in the range of less than 10 nm particles are known as carbon dots (CDs). Recently, carbon dots usually termed as quasi-spherical zero-dimensional nanomaterials, have shown peculiar potentials in various fields due to their optical properties, considerable biocompatibility, low toxicity, and photostability as well [1–3]. But, the metal-based semiconductor quantum dots cause serious health and environmental effects [4].

To date, copious sources worn to prepare CDs, pooled with top-down and bottom-up methodologies [5,6]. The top-down methods prepare CDs commencing larger carbon materials, such as nanodiamonds [7], graphite [8–11], carbon nanotubes [12,13], carbon soots [14], activated carbon [15], graphite oxide [16], while the bottom-up approaches synthesize CQDs from molecular precursors such as citric acid [17], glucose [18,19], and resin [20]. As a result of CDs surface chemistry, which is typically rich in carboxylic acid, alcohol and amine functionalities, CDs are hydrophilic, making them highly compatible with aqueous chemistry. They are generally considered to be environmentally benign and widely investigated for several applications. So, CDs take infront as capable alternative in targeted drug delivery [21], bioimaging and biomedicine [22–26], sensors [27], electrocatalysis [28–30] and photocatalysis [31]. In 2013, extremely blue fluorescent CDs were successfully primed, with a quantum yield in an aqueous solution similar to semiconductor QDs, marking a significant advance in this area [32]. Since then, attempts have been made on manifold applications of CDs in biomedicine and optoelectronics such as drug delivery [33], photodynamic therapy (PDT) [34], light emitting diodes (LEDs) [35] and solar cells [36].

However, the above mentioned methods were typically engrossing expensive instruments, vast solvent medium and/or time unpreserved. Hence pass up this, currently researchers pay attention in valuable, reliable, easier and solvent-free methodologies to acquire a large range of CDs for novel applications. In this study, for the first time, we produce carbon dots from 2-propanol (2P-CDs) based on a one-pot ingenious preparative method. This self-heating, rapid carbonization provided oxygen-containing 2P-CDs. Significantly, the as-synthesized 2P-CDs have low toxicity, offers enlarged avenues for various applications in bioimaging and afar.

2-propanol (C3H8O, 99%, Merck), diphosphorus pentoxide (P2O5, 97%, Loba), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2, 30%, Rankem) and sodium carbonate (Na2CO3, 99.5%, Rankem) were all used as received. Deionized water (conductivity 3.5 μΩ/cm2, Aquoion TBD 100 water system) was used for the whole experiment.

Typically, 5 g of diphosphorus pentoxide (P2O5) was taken in a 100 ml beaker; to this 3 ml 2-propanol was added and mixed well. Light brownish glue was obtained; to this 0.3 ml of hydrogen peroxide was progressively added. Mixture color was instantly changed into dark brown and volume also expanded due to foaming by spontaneous heat formed about 180 °C during the reaction. In this, 2-propanol is carbon precursor due to its low carbonization temperature, diphosphorus pentoxide act as carbonizer and hydrogen peroxide act as vigorous carbonization initiator. The self-engendered bubbles were produced to remove the excessive heat and utilized as the templates to restrict the size of the 2P-CDs. After 15 min, the reaction mixture was diluted by dropwise addition of 20 ml water under simple shaking, the brownish yellow supernatant after centrifugation (15000 rpm, 30 min, Remi RM12C) was neutralized by adding Na2CO3. Finally, it was dialyzed against deionized water through dialysis membrane (cellulose ester dialysis membrane, 1 KD MWCO) for 3 days, affording purified 2P-CDs.

Ultra-violet excitation spectra (Perkin Elmer Lambda 25-UV/Vis spectrometer), Fluorescence emission spectra (Perkin Elmer LS-45 Fluorescence Spectrometer), Fourier Transform Infrared (FT-IR) Spectra (KBr, 4000-200 cm−1, Impact 400D Nicholet Spectrometer), Zeta potential (Zetasizer, Malvern), X-Ray Diffraction (Seifert, X-ray diffractometer Siemens D500 Spectrometer, 20° ≤ 2θ ≤ 80°, Cu Kα radiation at 35 kV and 25 mA), MicroRaman measurements (HR 800, Jobin–Yvon, 1800 grooves mm−1 holographic grating, HeNe laser of 633 nm, focus ≈3 μm and depth resolution was 0.5 μm, output 5 mW), XPS measurements (Escalab 220i-XL, Thermo VG, U.K., Mg-K α source (1253.6 eV), spectra acquired with 100 eV pass energy) Surface analysis (high resolution transmission electron microscope), Elemental analysis (Energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy) and Selected Area Electron Diffraction patterns (HRTEM, EDS and SAED, JEOL JEM 2100 operating at 200 kV, 200-mesh C-coated Cu grid, Gatan Orious CCD camera (2K× 2K) for image recording), were used to analyze the 2P-CDs.

HeLa cells were purchased from the American Type Culture Collection (ATCC). The culture medium for cells consisted of Dulbecco's modified Eagle's medium (DMEM, Grand Island) supplemented with 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS, Invitrogen, Grand Island, NY) and 1% antibiotics (Invitrogen, Grand Island, NY) in a standard incubator (5% CO2 atmosphere at 30 °C).

HeLa cells were cultured by means of a recognized protocol. HeLa cells were placed at a concentration of 1×105 per well on a 96-well flat-bottomed culture plate and incubated with various concentrations of 2P-CDs (30, 60, 90, 120 and 150 μg mL−1) for 24 h at 37 °C by using 5% CO2 in a humidified incubator. The cell viability was evaluated by the 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) assay. The outcome is presented as a reduction in metabolic activity in percentage relative to the metabolic activity of control cells cultured in growth medium only (100%).

HeLa cells (1 × 104) were seeded into 4-well plates containing 25 mm diameter cover glass and cells were grown for 24 h at room temperature. Before treatment with 2P-CDs (20 μg mL−1), the cells were incubated for 30 min at 4 °C. After 2 h incubation with 2P-CDs, the cells were washed twice with Dulbecco's phosphate-buffered saline (D-PBS) for 5 min and then fixed to 4% paraformaldehyde. The coverslips from the four-well plates were placed into a glass slide solution was added (Vector Laboratories, Inc., CA, USA). The confocal images were obtained at an excitation wavelength of 488 nm by using LSM 510 (Carl Zeiss Inc., Berlin, Germany).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Calculation of PL quantum yield of 2P-CDs
ce:section.ce:para:
2P-CDs were dissolved in DI water and we have used quinine sulphate (QY 0.54 in 0.1 M H2SO4) as a reference. The quantum yield was calculated as follows:
Φ
2 P − C D s
=
Φ
R e f
∗
(
I
2 P − CDs
I
Ref
)
∗
(
η
2 P − CDs
2
η
Ref
2
)
∗
(
A
R e f
A
2 P − C D s
)
where Ф, I, η and A are the quantum yield of quinine sulphate, measured integrated emission intensity, the refractive index of the solvent and the optical density (1.33 for both water and H2SO4), respectively, of the reference and 2P-CDs. The PL emission and absorbance were measured at an excitation wavelength of 360 nm, and the absorbance was maintained under 0.1 to avoid re-absorption effects.

TEM images obviously disclose that the as-synthesized 2P-CDs are quasispherical, monodispersed, narrow size distribution between 1.7 nm and 3.2 nm with the maximum population at 2.3 nm and the average particle size is 2.5 ± 0.7 nm (Fig. 1 and b). Typical X-ray diffraction (XRD) profile of 2P-CDs shows a single broad peak centered at 22.48° corresponds to the (002) plane of carbon-based materials with the sp3 disorder (Fig. 2 b). The interlayer spacing (d value) of the 2P-CDs (3.95 Ao) is higher than that of the graphite (3.3 Ao). The increase in d value indicates an increase in amorphous nature attributed to the introduction of oxygen-containing groups [37]. The selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern of 2P-CDs is in good agreement with these results (inset of Fig. 1a). Both XRD and SAED patterns confirm the amorphous nature of 2P-CDs. From EDS measurements the C/O atomic ratio of the synthesized 2P-CDs is 26.51, which is clearly evident for a considerable quantity of oxygen atoms existing in its core (Fig. 4 a).

The surface state and composition of the synthesized 2P-CDs were analyzed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The XPS survey spectrum of the 2P-CDs shows C 1s and O 1s peaks were deconvoluted into three and two peaks respectively (Fig. 3a). The 2P-CDs high-resolution XPS spectrum of the C 1s peak (Fig. 3b) shows three types of carbon peaks at 284.8, 286.2, and 288.7 eV, which can be assigned to sp3-CC/sp2-CC, CO, and CO bonds on the surface respectively [38]. Further, the 2P-CDs high-resolution XPS spectrum O 1s peak shows two peaks at 531.2 and 532.6 eV which were assigned to CO and OC bonding respectively (Fig. 3c). Raman spectroscopy offers an essential tool to understand the structural and chemical changes of the carbon nanomaterials. The 2P-CDs microRaman spectrum shows two typical peaks around 1365 and 1592 cm−1 related to the D and G bands of amorphous carbon, respectively (Fig. 3d). The D band refers to the A 1g symmetry vibration mode of the sp2-bonded carbon atom, whereas the G band corresponds to the in-plane E 2g stretching vibration mode of single-crystal graphite that arises from the A 1g (zone-edge) breathing vibration phonon, which is activated only in the presence of a neighboring sp3 defect [39]. The D band and G band ratio (ID/IG) is 1.31 (in general ID/IG is less than 1 for well graphitized carbon), which is the characteristic of disorder extent and the ratio of sp3/sp2 carbon, inferring the structural defects in the 2P-CDs during the rapid carbonization process. The fluorescence of the synthesized 2P-CDs is associated with surface defects of the carbon skeleton that trap the excited-state energy [40]. Therefore, this Raman spectroscopy results proved that the synthesized 2P-CDs have an amorphous structure with sufficient major surface defects.

FTIR spectrum of the as-synthesized 2P-CDs is shown in Fig. 2a. This FTIR spectrum shows various peaks corresponding to groups such as ν(OH), ν(sp3 CH), ν(CO), δ(CH3 & CH2) deformation, ν(CO) and δ(sp3 CH) on the surface of 2P-CDs. The absorption bands of OH and sp3 CH stretching vibrations appear at 3370 cm−1 (broad) and 2957 cm−1 respectively. Similarly, the absorption bands of CO, CO stretching vibrations, and carbonization peaks appear at 1635 cm−1 (medium), 1000 cm−1 (strong) and 1150 cm−1 (strong) respectively. The absorption bands of CH3 & CH2 deformation and sp3 CH bending vibrations appear at 1450 cm−1 (medium) and 886 cm−1 (medium) respectively. Zeta potential value substantiates that, the as-synthesized 2P-CDs are negatively charged which is obvious for carboxyl and hydroxyl groups existing on its fringe (Fig. 4b).

The UV–Vis excitation and emission spectrum of the 2P-CDs shows one sharp peak around 300 nm which corresponds to n-π* transition of CO bonds (Fig. 5 a). Furthermore, the exclusive phenomenon of excitation dependent photoluminescence was observed in the synthesized 2P-CDs (Fig. 5b). The number of particles excited at a particular wavelength has a linear relationship with PL intensity. Due to the largest number of 2P-CDs particles being excited at 390 nm, the highest photoluminescence (PL) intensity was observed. Another anticipative reason for excitation dependent photoluminescence performance was due to the occurrence of excitons in carbon [13], aromatic conjugate structures [41], emissive traps [42], surface states [43] and free zig-zag sites [44]. Among these, surface states are the most important factor involves wavelength dependent PL. As evident from FTIR spectra, the synthesized 2P-CDs have various chemical bonding environments like ν(OH), ν(sp3 CH), ν(CO), δ(CH3 & CH2) deformation, ν(CO) and δ(sp3 CH) groups which may result in series of emissive traps dominates certain excitation wavelength, respectively. The quantum yield of 2P-CDs is about 15% in comparison with quinine sulphate (QY 0.54 in 0.1 M H2SO4).

Cells were cultured and maintained in DMEM medium containing 2P-CDs as per the procedure described in materials section. The possible application of as-synthesized 2P-CDs as a cell-imaging agent was explored. The inherent in vitro cytotoxicity of 2P-CDs was evaluated using HeLa cell lines through MTT assay. Our 2P-CDs did not impose any significant toxicity to cells and are bearable even at high prescribed amount (150 μg mL−1) (Fig. 6 ).

Confocal images of 2P-CDs treated HeLa cells with series of emissions like blue (465–486), green (508–540), yellow (561–604) and red (636–700) excited at wavelength 488 nm; bright field and merged confocal images are shown in Fig. 7 a–f. The confocal image of HeLa cells at λex = 488 nm clearly shows the agglomeration of fluorescent 2P-CDs inside the cells leaving clear zones of nuclei. Furthermore, the excitation-dependent luminescence of synthesized 2P-CDs gives rise to several visible consequences in imaging. When the laser excitation was changed to 405 nm fluorescent blue color was not observed. All these preludes demonstrate that the 2P-CDs obtained from 2-propanol can serve as a budding alternate for organic dyes or semiconductor QDs in bioimaging.

In conclusion, we have demonstrated a cost-effective, one-pot ingenious preparative method for synthesis 2P-CDs without external treatments rapidly produced by simple mixing. These amorphous 2P-CDs show strong and stable fluorescence which is dependent on excitation wavelength. The as-synthesized 2P-CDs do not show any cytotoxicity and are eco-friendly taken up by the active cells. Due to the combined advantages of facile synthesis, high stability, good and tunable luminescence and high biocompatibility, these 2P-CDs can promise potential applications in biolabeling as well as solution state optoelectronics.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Raja Angamuthu acknowledges Periyar University for University Research Fellowship (URF, Ref: PU/A&A-3/URF/2014)
